title: Washington’s quantum mobilisation: from discovery to deployment
author: IISS
text:
Dongyoun Cho Senior Fellow for Advanced Technologies, National Security and DefenceAbout Dongyoun

Online Analysis9th July 2026

Washington’s quantum mobilisation: from discovery to deploymentIn the space of two weeks in June, the US Congress, the White House, the Pentagon and the Department of Commerce all made key decisions on quantum technology. The sequence marks a deliberate shift: Washington is no longer waiting for the science to settle before building the infrastructure to deploy it.

In the second half of June 2026, the United States produced its most concentrated burst of quantum policymaking since the 2018 National Quantum Initiative Act. On 15 June, a bipartisan bill – the National Security Commission Quantum Computing Act of 2026 – was introduced in the House. On 22 June, the White House issued two executive orders: Executive Order 14412, ‘Securing the Nation Against Advanced Cryptographic Attacks’, accelerating the federal transition to post-quantum cryptography (PQC); and Executive Order 14413, ‘Ushering in the Next Frontier of Quantum Innovation’, directing a whole-of-government push on deployment and commercialisation. The following day, the Department of War released its first department-wide PQC strategy. The Office of Management and Budget followed on 24 June with the implementing memorandum. On 29 June, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) announced a partnership with SRI International to establish a Quantum Manufacturing Engineering Center (QMEC).
Read individually, each measure is incremental. Read together, they enact a strategic reorientation. The science hasn’t changed, but the sequencing has. The flagship computing initiative in Executive Order 14413 aims to initiate the era of quantum-enabled scientific discovery, rather than to conclude it. Rather than waiting for laboratory maturity before addressing manufacturing, procurement, security and workforce questions, Washington is now mobilising these tracks simultaneously. Deployment infrastructure is being built in parallel with the science it is meant to deliver. This matters because, as argued in March, the decisive phase of quantum competition will be infrastructural rather than experimental. The question is no longer simply who achieves the next technical milestone, but who can govern the supply chains, institutions and standards through which quantum systems will operate. Two tests are likely to determine whether states could convert research leadership into strategic capability: governance of specialised supply chains and institutional capacity to absorb new systems. June’s measures are Washington’s first systematic attempt to answer both.

Answering the supply-chain test
The clearest signal comes from the most prosaic initiative. QMEC, funded by NIST and managed by SRI International, will focus its initial work not on qubits (the basic units of quantum information) or algorithms, but on cryostats and lasers – the ultra-cold refrigeration and precision-light systems without which quantum sensors and computers cannot function, let alone scale. Its stated early goal is to advance the manufacturability of quantum-enabling components within three years, alongside building a US-based supply chain for the materials and components required by quantum systems.
This is a direct policy response to the vulnerability that analytical tools, such as the Quantum Criticality Index, have highlighted: that highly specialised inputs produced in small volumes for a narrow market – advanced cryogenics, precision photonics, isotopically enriched materials – function as strategic choke points when concentrated supply meets tightening export controls. Disruption to the single supplier of dilution refrigerators or stabilised laser systems can delay national programmes, regardless of how many qubits a laboratory can demonstrate.
Executive Order 14413 embeds the same logic at the level of national strategy. Its supply-chain provisions direct the Department of Commerce to analyse quantum supply chains and eliminate manufacturing barriers; instruct the Department of War to widen domestic access to quantum information science and technology (QIST)-relevant foundry resources (semiconductor-fabrication facilities); and task agencies with designing incentive mechanisms – potentially including prize challenges and advance market commitments – to develop quantum-enabling component technologies in the US. The order also mandates government-wide sharing of supply-chain intelligence, including data generated under the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency Quantum Benchmarking Initiative.
Moreover, a month earlier, the Department of Commerce signed letters of intent worth just over US$2 billion in proposed CHIPS and Science Act incentives for quantum foundries and quantum-computing firms. Taken together with QMEC and the executive order’s component-technology provisions, the US is assembling what amounts to an industrial policy for the quantum-supply base, something that until recently existed only for semiconductors.
Answering the absorption test
The second test concerns whether institutions can actually integrate what laboratories produce into their procurement, standards and operational workflows. Here, Executive Order 14412 is the more consequential. It converts the long-discussed ‘harvest now, decrypt later’ threat into enforceable deadlines: federal high-impact systems must adopt PQC for key establishment (the negotiation of shared encryption keys) by the end of 2030 and for digital signatures by the end of 2031, with compliance obligations extending to government contractors through NIST-approved standards.
The speed of institutional follow-through is telling. The Department of War published its PQC strategy one day after the orders were signed; the document carries an April 2026 clearance date and appears to have been timed to land alongside the executive actions. It builds on a November 2025 directive requiring component-level cryptographic inventories and a named official responsible for migration in each component, and frames the migration as stripping vulnerable algorithms out of the entire data pathway, not simply adding new ones. On 24 June, the Office of Management and Budget completed the administrative machinery with Memorandum M-26-15, ‘Execution of the Migration to Post-Quantum Cryptography’, the first federal road map to set a phased migration schedule, running in five stages to 2035, with requirements for cryptographic inventories, migration plans and cryptographic bills of materials. The executive order had allowed 90 days for implementation guidance. It arrived in two. Together, the two documents recalibrate the federal timeline. The migration horizon of 2035, which has guided federal planning since 2022, remains the end point of the roadmap, not the deadline for high-impact systems, which is now 2030–31.
Whether these deadlines hold is a separate question; large-scale cryptographic migration across legacy federal systems has slipped before, and the orders are subject to Congressional funding decisions. But the institutional architecture of hard dates, named responsibilities, procurement leverage over contractors and department-level strategies is precisely what absorption capacity looks like in practice. In March, it was suggested that the pace of PQC migration would be an early indicator of which governance model was working. Within three months, Washington has written its answer into the federal calendar.
The NSCAI grammar
If the executive orders show the administration consolidating, the congressional track shows something subtler: Washington is beginning to process quantum through the same institutional ‘grammar’ (the rules by which it assembles its response) that it applied to artificial intelligence (AI).
The 2026 National Security Commission Quantum Computing Act, introduced by Representatives Mike Lawler and Pat Ryan, would establish an 11-member independent commission funded through the defence budget, appointed by the secretary of defense (now secretary of war) and the leadership of the House and Senate Armed Services Committees, reporting within 180 days and annually thereafter until a 2030 sunset. Anyone who followed the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (NSCAI) will recognise the template immediately: it mirrors, almost line for line, the body that shaped US AI policy between 2018 and 2021, and whose final report seeded much of the current AI-competition agenda.
The substance follows the form. The commission’s mandate covers competitiveness, foreign investment and workforce, but also the risks of military employment of quantum computing – including implications under the law of armed conflict and for escalation dynamics – and the ethical questions raised by future applications. These are the questions a state asks when it expects a technology to become operational, not merely promising.
The bill remains a proposal; commissions are introduced more often than they are established, and its provisions may yet be folded into broader legislation such as the National Quantum Initiative Reauthorization Act, currently moving through the Senate – itself framed around sustaining federal investment beyond basic research and into domestic manufacturing and deployment, the same infrastructural turn visible across the executive measures. But the signal does not depend on enactment. A bipartisan pairing of a Republican and a Democrat – the latter a member of the United States House Committee on Armed Services – has placed quantum computing on an institutional trajectory similar to that of AI eight years earlier. The precedent suggests what comes next: sustained Congressional attention, a defence-anchored policy agenda and, eventually, budget lines.
The military timeline: quantum sensing first
The most concrete military commitment in June’s measures involves neither cryptography nor computing. Executive Order 14413 directs the secretary of war to identify at least three next-generation quantum-sensor projects and to field them by 30 September 2028, an operational deadline barely two years away.
The choice of domain is instructive. Quantum sensing sits furthest along the maturity curve: quantum clocks, gravimeters and magnetometers offer navigation and detection capabilities that do not depend on fault-tolerant computing and can be integrated into existing platforms. A fielding mandate with a fiscal-year deadline indicates that parts of the US defence establishment now treat quantum sensing as a procurement rather than a research problem. Militarisation, in other words, is arriving through sensors before computers, with implications for undersea detection, navigation in GPS-denied environments and the survivability of stealth platforms that strategic communities have only begun to work through.
The protective architecture is expanding in step with the operational push. The order directs an expansion of the Quantum Information Science and Technology Counterintelligence Protection Team to coordinate defences against adversarial threats across industry and academia, and requires agencies to channel all security-related outreach to the quantum sector through that team, so that companies and universities hear a single coordinated message. Combined with the direction to establish or designate a centre for national-security applications of quantum computing, the pattern is one of simultaneous acceleration and enclosure: pushing the technology towards operational use while tightening the perimeter around it.
What this means beyond Washington
For allies and partners, the June measures sharpen a choice that was already forming. Executive Order 14413’s international provisions direct the US Department of State and US Department of Commerce to harmonise investment restrictions and export controls with ‘like-minded countries’, to keep critical quantum-enabling technologies away from countries of concern, and to align existing frameworks with these priorities – including Pax Silica, the State Department’s economic–security coalition on AI and technology supply chains. Launched in December 2025, the coalition added the European Union, Germany and the Netherlands among ten new signatories at its second summit on 25–26 June, days after the order was signed. Access to the US quantum ecosystem, in other words, will increasingly be conditioned on regulatory alignment with it.
This raises the stakes for Europe in particular. The EU’s upcoming Quantum Act and the 2025 Quantum Europe Strategy were conceived largely as instruments of industrial scaling-up and coordination. They must now also function as instruments of alignment, determining whether European suppliers of cryogenics, photonics and enabling components sit inside or outside the trusted networks that Washington and its export-control partners are constructing. For states in Asia and elsewhere that are navigating between US and Chinese technology ecosystems, the trajectory points towards the consolidation of what would amount to a US-led quantum bloc with explicit membership conditions. If it materialises, hedging will become progressively more expensive and the room for it narrower. That bloc does not yet exist in treaty form, but the regulatory scaffolding for it is now being assembled: harmonised export controls and investment screening, mutual recognition of standards and certification, and shared access to trusted foundry and testbed infrastructure.
Three things are worth watching over the coming year. Firstly, implementation: whether the executive orders’ deadlines survive contact with appropriations cycles and agency capacity. Secondly, the commission bill’s fate and whether its defence-anchored framing pulls congressional quantum policy further into the national-security lane. Thirdly, the responses: Beijing’s quantum programme has long operated on a mobilisation logic of its own, and while no specific countermoves to June’s measures have yet surfaced, the pattern of earlier technology contests suggests that they will; Brussels, meanwhile, must decide how much of its regulatory energy to spend on alignment rather than autonomy.
The deeper shift, however, is already legible. In March, the open question was whether any of the three models of quantum statecraft could pass the infrastructure tests that would decide this phase of competition. This analysis has deliberately traced only the American answer; Beijing and Europe’s response deserves further examination. What the US has now shown is how it intends to compete – not with the next breakthrough, but with a new sequence, refusing to let deployment wait for the science.

AuthorDongyoun Cho Senior Fellow for Advanced Technologies, National Security and Defence
